food_subgroup: Cooked chicken dishes — spiced and herb-based curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pudina pepper chicken. FOODON_00002427 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for spiced mint-pepper chicken preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to pudina pepper chicken. No suitable parent concept identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of pudina pepper chicken is approximately 250 g, comprising boneless chicken cubes in a mint-pepper sauce, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, homemade — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), adapted for mint-pepper chicken preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for chicken curry (boneless, cooked with spices and oil). Pudina pepper chicken is a closely analogous preparation — boneless chicken cooked with spices, oil, and aromatics. Mint leaves contribute negligible macronutrient change. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry entry: energy ~165 kcal/100g, protein ~18g, fat ~9g, carbohydrate ~3g. These are consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken curry (FDC ID 174957) used as cross-reference validation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pudina pepper chicken. Chicken curry dishes are predominantly protein and fat with very low carbohydrate content (~3g/100g). The University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) classify low-carbohydrate meat-based dishes as GI ≤ 40 (low). A GI of 35 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate consistent with similar spiced chicken preparations. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (35 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.8, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100g; ~800 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size as a 250g restaurant serving contributes approximately 800 mg sodium (~35% of the 2300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced salt preparation or limiting to a half-portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.2g/100g) and low glycemic index (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pudina pepper chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; it provides high-quality protein that supports satiety without causing significant postprandial glucose excursions when consumed without high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.4g/100g) and sodium (~320 mg/100g) from cooking oil and spice base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to combined saturated fat and sodium load; choosing lean chicken breast, minimal added oil, and requesting low-salt preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk while retaining the dish's high protein benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pudina Pepper Chicken is a North Indian spiced chicken dish featuring boneless chicken cubes cooked with freshly ground black pepper, mint leaves (pudina), onion, ginger, garlic, green chilli, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The dish is prepared by sautéing aromatics in oil, adding marinated chicken, and finishing with crushed black pepper and fresh mint to create a bold, herbaceous, and peppery flavour profile. The texture is tender and succulent with a moderately thick, fragrant sauce. Pudina pepper chicken is a popular menu item at North Indian restaurants in Singapore, including Curry Village and Bombay Curry And Kebabs. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish (~18g protein, ~3.2g carbohydrate per 100g) with a low glycemic index (~35), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium content is moderate (~320 mg/100g) and should be considered by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal-certified in Singapore restaurant contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pudina pepper chicken?
Is pudina pepper chicken good for diabetics?
What does pudina mean in Indian food?
Is pudina pepper chicken gluten free?
Mint pepper chicken North Indian recipe

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry data (2017), which is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. No exact entry for pudina pepper chicken exists in any approved primary source. Cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central chicken curry entry (FDC ID 174957) confirms macro plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database classification framework and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement is available.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) chicken curry entry and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174957. Mint leaves contribute negligible macronutrient change to the base chicken curry profile. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for pudina pepper chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Serving size of 250g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant portion norms in Singapore. Hindi alias पुदीना मुर्ग (Pudina Murgh) is included in aliases_by_language hindi and the Devanagari form पुदीना मुर्ग is also present in the flat aliases list to maintain consistency.
